writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial psychological thriller, blending obsessive-procedural tension with soap-opera domestic fallout, in the vein of prestige ensemble TV
style_guidelines: Every scene should feel like it's overheard, not narrated at — let dialogue carry subtext instead of characters stating their feelings outright.
The killer's clues should read as genuinely clever and unsettling, never as lazy riddles solved by convenient leaps of logic.
Domestic scenes (the Vance marriage) deserve the same craft and stakes as procedural scenes — a quiet dinner-table silence can carry as much dread as a crime scene.
Let institutional politics (captain, partner, precinct) breathe as their own texture — bureaucracy, favors, and reputations matter as much as the case itself.
Escalate psychological unease gradually: Elias's moral compromises should feel like small, justifiable steps at the time, only clearly damning in hindsight.
Never let a character's dialogue reveal knowledge that belongs to another character's private thread — misunderstandings must arise honestly from what each person actually knows.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

ELIAS_VANCE.default_style: sharp, controlled, a detective's precision fraying at the edges under obsession
ELIAS_VANCE.description: a brilliant homicide detective drawn into a killer's psychological game; his hunger to win it slowly erodes his own ethics, and he does not see how far he has already fallen

MIRA_VANCE.default_style: warm but increasingly guarded, speaks slow and deliberate, very clear English
MIRA_VANCE.description: Elias's wife, a Korean-born family therapist who emigrated in her twenties; she senses her husband disappearing into the case but misreads the distance as infidelity rather than obsession

CAPTAIN_ZHOU.default_style: gruff authority softened by genuine affection for his protégé, speaks clear and unhurried English
CAPTAIN_ZHOU.description: the precinct captain, a first-generation immigrant who rose through the ranks and mentored Elias personally; torn between institutional loyalty and a mounting suspicion that his best detective is compromised

PARTNER_LIN.default_style: eager, quick-tongued, gradually curdling into wary suspicion
PARTNER_LIN.description: Elias's young partner, raised in a Chinese immigrant household; she idolizes him at first and is the first to notice the small procedural lies he tells himself

SIMON_BORU.default_style: soft-spoken, unhurried, courteous in a way that never quite feels safe
SIMON_BORU.description: an elderly antiquarian bookshop owner, an immigrant scholar with a decades-old grievance no one else knows about; the killer, though nothing in his public manner suggests it

HAN_MEI.default_style: proud, plainspoken, increasingly frightened and defiant
HAN_MEI.description: a Beijing-born grocery store owner whose ordinary life is destroyed when the killer's planted evidence and Elias's tunnel vision make him the season's convenient suspect

PRECINCT.name: Ninth District Precinct
PRECINCT.description: a fluorescent-lit bullpen choked with corkboards, red string, and coffee-stained case files under a permanent hum of overworked ceiling fans

VANCE_HOME.name: The Vance House
VANCE_HOME.description: a warm suburban home slowly going cold — family photographs on every wall, one side of the bed increasingly, conspicuously undisturbed

BOOKSHOP.name: Boru's Rare Books
BOOKSHOP.description: a cramped antiquarian bookshop thick with dust and lamplight, shelves leaning close overhead, and a locked back room no customer has ever seen

DOCKS.name: The Tannery Docks
DOCKS.description: a rusted industrial waterfront wrapped in river fog, shipping containers stacked like a maze, chosen again and again as the killer's stage

DINER.name: Ruby's All-Night Diner
DINER.description: a neon-lit 24-hour diner with cracked red vinyl booths, the one place detectives go to say what they can't say at the precinct

HAN_GROCERY.name: Han's Grocery
HAN_GROCERY.description: a small, warmly lit corner grocery with family photos taped behind the register, its windows increasingly marked by graffiti as suspicion spreads

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: In a city where the fog off the docks never quite lifts, a brilliant detective receives a postcard with no return address — a single line of verse, and a promise that more will follow. What begins as an irresistible intellectual challenge becomes something else entirely: a season-long descent in which the hunter forgets, one compromise at a time, that he was never the one setting the terms of the game.
series_outro: The killer's true design comes fully into the light — a masterwork completed not by his own hand, but by the detective he spent months quietly shaping into an instrument. What remains afterward is left deliberately unresolved: a marriage too damaged to say for certain whether it survives, a precinct that must decide what to do with a detective it can no longer fully trust, and a wrongly accused man whose name is cleared on paper long before it is cleared in the eyes of his neighbors.

episodes.theme: Elias receives the killer's first cryptic clue and is instantly captivated by the intellectual challenge, while Mira quietly notes the first cracks in his attention at home and Han's grocery is glimpsed as unremarkable background about to be anything but (The Puppeteer's Game and The Vance Marriage both begin, no resolution yet).
episodes.outro_note: Alone at his desk long after the bullpen empties, Elias finally cracks the verse's hidden reference — and feels an unwelcome chill at how precisely it was built to lead him, and only him, exactly here.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game
case.solution: Simon Boru, the quiet antiquarian bookseller Elias has come to trust as a source on obscure texts, is the killer behind the postcards and the Tannery Docks murders. Decades ago, Boru's son died in prison after a wrongful conviction that the department — including Elias's own mentor — quietly let stand rather than reopen an inconvenient case; Boru has spent years since planning not merely a string of murders but a slow, deliberate remaking of a brilliant detective into someone capable of the same moral trespasses the system committed against him. Every clue is engineered to flatter Elias's intellect while nudging him toward cutting corners, and the final 'masterwork' Boru intends is not a murder he commits himself, but one he maneuvers Elias into committing.
objective_facts: A body was found at the Tannery Docks with a hand-delivered postcard addressed personally to Detective Elias Vance left at the scene.
The postcard contains a single line of verse and no return address, and forensics finds no usable prints or fibers on it.
Simon Boru's late son, Daniel Boru, died in state custody nine years ago following a conviction Simon has never publicly disputed.
Every clue left by the killer references an obscure text that can be sourced to stock once carried by Boru's Rare Books.

knows: The postcard was addressed to him by name and left deliberately at a fresh crime scene
The verse on the postcard references an obscure text he doesn't yet recognize
hides: That the postcard was addressed to him personally — he hasn't told Captain Zhou, wanting to solve it before it becomes 'his' case in a way that invites oversight
believes_falsely: That the postcard is simply an unusually bold piece of evidence left by an attention-seeking killer, rather than a message engineered specifically to draw him into a private contest

knows: A cryptic postcard addressed to Elias personally was recovered from the scene and logged as evidence
hides: Her mild reluctance to press Elias on why he's keeping the postcard's full wording to himself, not wanting to seem like she's overstepping this early in their partnership
believes_falsely: That Elias is simply being thorough by keeping his notes close, not that he's already begun working the case alone in ways he won't share

knows: Every detail of the postcard's verse, and exactly which obscure text it points to, because he chose and placed it himself
hides: That he is the author of the postcard and the murder, presenting himself to Elias as nothing more than a helpful bookseller
believes_falsely: That grooming Elias into someone capable of his own trespasses will finally quiet his own grief — a certainty his meticulous planning has never actually tested

case.label: The Vance Marriage
case.solution: There is no affair. Elias's growing distance from Mira is entirely the product of the killer's private game consuming his attention and his nights; he keeps it from her not out of guilt over infidelity but out of a mistaken belief that shielding her from the case protects their marriage, and that silence reads to Mira as exactly the kind of guilt he isn't feeling.
objective_facts: Elias has not discussed the postcard, the verse, or any detail of the killer's contact with Mira.
Elias has stayed at the precinct or driven to the docks alone on multiple nights this week without telling Mira where he was.
Mira has noticed Elias checking his phone and stepping out of rooms to take calls he doesn't explain.

knows: He is not being unfaithful to Mira
He has been staying away from home later than usual because of the case
hides: The existence and contents of the postcard, and how much time he's spending chasing it alone, because he tells himself she has enough to carry with her own patients
believes_falsely: That keeping Mira in the dark is protecting her, when in fact the silence itself is what's frightening her

knows: Elias has been distant, secretive about his phone, and out at odd hours without explanation
hides: Her growing unease from Elias directly, choosing to watch and wait rather than confront him outright
believes_falsely: That the pattern of secrecy and distance most likely points to another woman, since Elias has never shut her out like this over a case before

episodes.theme: Elias cracks the hidden meaning of the first clue and chooses to keep the killer's game as a private obsession rather than share it with his partner, while Mira notices he has started keeping secrets that have nothing to do with paperwork (The Puppeteer's Game escalates, The Vance Marriage strain deepens).
episodes.intro_note: Elias closes out his first night having cracked the postcard's opening reference, unsettled by how deliberately it seemed aimed at him — a feeling he tells no one, not even Mira, who has already begun to notice the distance opening between them.
episodes.outro_note: The next volume Boru presses into his hands falls open, unprompted, to a page already worn soft at exactly the passage Elias needs — and for one unguarded second he wonders how a stranger's bookshop could possibly know his mind that well, before he shakes the thought off, buttons his coat, and heads out into the fog toward whatever the docks are about to show him.

episodes.theme: A second clue leads the investigation toward Han's grocery store, and Elias's eagerness to chase the connection plants the season's first seed of a wrongful suspicion (The Puppeteer's Game advances, The Frame is introduced).
episodes.intro_note: The uncanny ease of that borrowed book still nags at him, but Elias buries the thought under the pull of the case — and when a matchbook from a quiet neighborhood grocery turns up near the docks, he follows the thread without once asking why it was left somewhere so easy to find.
episodes.outro_note: Over Lin's objections and without Han's alibi ever properly checked, Elias walks into Zhou's office and asks for a warrant — certain, for the first time in the case, that he's finally ahead of the game, when every version of that certainty so far has belonged to someone else.

case.label: The Frame
case.solution: Han Mei is entirely innocent. The matchbook and receipt tying him to the Tannery Docks were planted by Simon Boru specifically because an ordinary, hardworking grocer with no real connection to the murders makes a far more convincing scapegoat than the truth — and because feeding Elias a lead he believes he found himself is the next step in eroding his judgment. Han's own nervousness during questioning, born of an old, unrelated brush with immigration paperwork trouble years ago, reads to Elias as guilt rather than fear.
objective_facts: A matchbook from Han's Grocery and a receipt bearing Han's handwriting were recovered near the Tannery Docks crime scene.
Neither item places Han Mei physically at the docks at the time of the murder.
Han Mei has no criminal record and a documented alibi for at least one of the nights in question that has not yet been seriously checked.
Han Mei had a minor, unrelated immigration paperwork issue years ago that he has never disclosed to police out of fear it will be used against him.

believes_falsely: That Han's nervousness is a sign of guilt about the murders, when it's actually fear rooted in something unrelated and years old

believes_falsely: That there's probably something more solid backing Elias's certainty that simply hasn't made it into the file yet

believes_falsely: That simply telling the truth about not knowing anything will be enough to clear him quickly, not realizing how his fear-driven evasiveness reads as guilt

episodes.theme: Elias bends procedure to keep pace with the killer's timetable, drawing Captain Zhou's first quiet concern while the case against an increasingly cornered Han hardens without real proof (The Puppeteer's Game, The Frame, institutional doubt all escalate together).
episodes.intro_note: The second clue led straight to Han's Grocery, and Elias, hungry for a lead, let suspicion outrun proof. Now the investigation tightens around a man whose only crime may be convenience.
episodes.outro_note: Elias gets his warrant. He has barely finished signing it when a new postcard arrives at the house, and its final line makes unmistakably clear that whoever is writing them was watching him fill out that very form.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game
case.solution: Simon Boru, whose son was killed years ago by a violent offender the justice system quietly failed to properly punish, has spent a year secretly studying Elias Vance before selecting him as the instrument of a private philosophical experiment: proof that even the most principled enforcer of justice can be turned, one small compromise at a time, into a killer himself. Boru is committing a series of staged murders, seeding each scene with cryptic literary clues drawn from rare antiquarian texts sold only through his own shop, deliberately calibrated to be solvable by a mind like Elias's and no other, while quietly manipulating physical evidence to implicate an innocent bystander. The endgame is not the killings themselves but the final act he intends to engineer: Elias, believing he is stopping or avenging something, will be maneuvered into killing Boru's true final target with his own hands, completing Boru's masterwork by someone else's conscience.
objective_facts: Every clue left at a crime scene traces back to rare antiquarian volumes and restoration materials handled exclusively through Boru's shop.
Trace evidence at each scene bears a distinctive archival-grade paper and ink that Boru privately restores in his back room.
Boru cultivated an anonymous correspondence with Elias for nearly a year before the first postcard arrived, though Elias does not yet know this.

believes_falsely: She believes his secrecy points to an affair, since he offers no other explanation and refuses to discuss the case with her at all.

case.label: The Frame
case.solution: Han Mei is entirely innocent. Boru planted ordinary items purchased at Han's own grocery, along with fabricated location-based evidence, to falsely tie him to the second crime scene, exploiting the fact that anyone can walk into an open store and buy anything. Elias's eagerness for a fast resolution causes him to treat thin circumstantial evidence as far stronger than it is.
objective_facts: The packaging and twine recovered at the second crime scene are common brands sold to dozens of Han's customers every week.
Han was seen near the Tannery Docks the same week purely because of his routine weekly supply run, unrelated to the murder.
No physical evidence recovered so far ties Han directly to the victim; everything gathered is circumstantial or location-based.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game
case.solution: Simon Boru, whose son was killed years ago by a violent offender the justice system quietly failed to properly punish, has spent a year secretly studying Elias Vance before selecting him as the instrument of a private philosophical experiment: proof that even the most principled enforcer of justice can be turned, one small compromise at a time, into a killer himself. Boru is committing a series of staged murders, seeding each scene with cryptic literary clues drawn from rare antiquarian texts sold only through his own shop, deliberately calibrated to be solvable by a mind like Elias's and no other, while quietly manipulating physical evidence to implicate an innocent bystander. The endgame is not the killings themselves but the final act he intends to engineer: Elias, believing he is stopping or avenging something, will be maneuvered into killing Boru's true final target with his own hands, completing Boru's masterwork by someone else's conscience.

episodes.theme: A new clue very nearly exposes the killer's identity, but Elias's certainty about Han blinds him to the moment slipping past, pushing The Frame toward crisis just as The Puppeteer's Game tightens its grip (Escalation peaks ahead of the season's midpoint turn).
episodes.intro_note: Mira followed him into the fog off the docks and watched him vanish into a bookshop she's never heard him mention, then watched him come out again an hour later, saying nothing. She still doesn't know what she saw. Neither, yet, does he know she was there.
episodes.outro_note: Elias holds the one piece of paper that could have ended this the right way, and folds it back into the case file as a footnote, because it doesn't fit the story he's already decided is true. Then he picks up his phone and gives the order to bring Han in.

episodes.theme: Han's wrongful arrest detonates through the precinct and the Vance household alike in the season's biggest turn yet, as Mira learns just enough of what Elias has done to shatter her trust in him (MIDPOINT TURN: The Frame crisis, The Vance Marriage upheaval, and The Puppeteer's Game collide).
episodes.intro_note: The clue Elias nearly missed in the small hours points, at last, somewhere concrete — and by dawn, that certainty has a name, a storefront, and a set of handcuffs waiting.
episodes.outro_note: Han Mei sits in a holding cell insisting on his innocence to walls that don't answer. In Boru's Rare Books, a new note waits to be found, addressed to a detective who no longer knows he's being led. And in a house gone quiet, Mira Vance packs a bag she hasn't decided yet whether she's taking out the door.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game
case.solution: Simon Boru, terminally ill and once a promising young criminologist broken and discredited decades ago by an institution he trusted, has spent months as the season's serial killer, engineering murders as a philosophical 'masterwork' about how systems corrupt good men. Every postcard, clue, and near-miss was designed not to be solved by Elias alone, but to walk him step by step toward a final act: killing Simon himself in what will look like a justified shooting, so that Elias — not Simon — becomes the hand that completes the masterwork, proving Simon's thesis and making the detective his unwitting successor.
objective_facts: Simon Boru has terminal pancreatic cancer with only months to live.
Every clue left for Elias was authored and placed by Simon personally, timed to his own schedule, not a real investigative trail.
Simon was once a criminology researcher who was quietly discredited and dismissed decades ago after a professional betrayal by a mentor at the precinct's founding generation.
Simon has never directly killed anyone himself in this case; each death was staged to implicate someone else while Simon orchestrated from a distance.
Simon's final design requires Elias himself to commit a fatal act against Simon, believing it justified.

knows: The clue that arrived tonight uses the same handwriting and paper stock as every prior postcard

believes_falsely: She still assumes the clue-writer and the actual killer must be two separate people working in concert, not the same man

case.label: The Frame
case.solution: Simon planted physical evidence connecting Han Mei's grocery to two of the murder scenes, exploiting the fact that Han had been secretly buying discounted, undocumented imported goods from a supplier to keep his struggling store afloat — a real but unrelated illegality that made Han evasive and physically present near the docks at hours he couldn't explain to police, giving Elias's tunnel-visioned case against him just enough true detail to feel solid.
objective_facts: The physical evidence found at Han's grocery, a victim's watch and matching fibers, was planted there by Simon Boru, not left by Han.
Han Mei has been receiving undocumented, under-the-table shipments of discounted goods from a dock supplier to keep his grocery solvent, requiring him to be at the docks at odd hours.
Han Mei has no connection whatsoever to any of the murders.
Elias built the case against Han primarily on circumstantial proximity and Han's evasiveness, not on solid direct evidence.

knows: He has never harmed anyone and has no idea why police evidence points to him

hides: The existence of his under-the-table supplier deal, even now, for fear it will be used against him on top of the murder charge
believes_falsely: He believes a rival grocer or a disgruntled former employee must be behind the planted evidence, not a stranger he's never met

knows: The physical evidence found in Han's storeroom

hides: A nagging awareness that the evidence arrived almost too conveniently, which he suppresses rather than investigates
believes_falsely: He believes the arrest is justified and that Han's evasiveness is guilt rather than an unrelated secret

knows: The chain of custody on the planted watch has a gap Elias glossed over
hides: That she flagged the gap once already tonight and was overruled
believes_falsely: She isn't yet certain the case is actually wrong, only that it was built too fast

knows: The DA wants an arrest tonight regardless of remaining gaps in the evidence
hides: His own doubts about the strength of the case from the DA, to protect Elias and the precinct's public standing
believes_falsely: He believes Elias's read on Han is probably right, because Elias has rarely been wrong before

case.label: The Vance Marriage
case.solution: Elias has told no one, including Mira, about the postcards or the private game he believes he's playing with the killer, both because Zhou asked him to keep it contained and because some part of him wants to solve it alone; his silence and late nights are entirely about the case, but Mira — finding fragments of postcards and cryptic notes without context — has concluded he is having an affair, a wound that hardens into genuine, independent distance of her own.
objective_facts: Elias has never been unfaithful to Mira at any point this season.
Elias has deliberately withheld all details of the postcard game from Mira since the first episode.
Mira found a torn postcard fragment and a hotel matchbook from a stakeout in Elias's coat, which she has not shown him.

hides: The postcards and the entire private game from Mira, believing he is protecting her by keeping it contained
believes_falsely: He believes his silence reads to Mira as work stress, not betrayal

case.label: Institutional Doubt

objective_facts: Partner Lin has kept a private written log of at least six procedural violations by Elias since the case began.
Captain Zhou has received direct pressure from the District Attorney's office to produce a named suspect before the arrest of Han Mei.
Han Mei's arrest was leaked to the press within hours, triggering public backlash before formal charges were even filed.
Zhou authorized Han's arrest despite his own private reservations about the strength of the evidence.

hides: The existence of the log itself, from both Elias and Zhou
believes_falsely: She still believes that if she raises her concerns clearly enough, Elias will course-correct on his own

knows: The DA's office pressured him directly to produce a named suspect before tonight's press cycle
hides: The full extent of that pressure from both Elias and Lin, preferring to absorb it himself
believes_falsely: He believes he can still contain the fallout quietly if Han's case holds even partially

knows: Zhou authorized the arrest despite incomplete evidence
hides: That he pushed Zhou harder than procedure allowed to get that authorization
believes_falsely: He believes the ends will justify the means once the real killer is caught

episodes.theme: With Han's case collapsing under public scrutiny, Elias digs deeper into the killer's game to justify what he's already done, forcing Partner Lin to choose between loyalty and conscience (The Frame begins to unravel, institutional doubt reaches crisis, The Puppeteer's Game intensifies).
episodes.intro_note: Days later, the city has an opinion about Han Mei's arrest, and none of the people closest to Elias Vance can agree anymore on who he actually is.
episodes.outro_note: Captain Zhou closes his office door on a detective he no longer fully trusts. Somewhere in the dark of Boru's back room, the next envelope is already sealed — and it has Elias Vance's name on it, waiting for the one clue he won't be able to walk away from.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game

Every postcard, clue, and near-miss was designed not to be solved by Elias alone, but to walk him step by step toward a final act: killing Simon himself in what will look like a justified shooting, so that Elias — not Simon — becomes the hand that completes the masterwork, proving Simon's thesis and making the detective his unwitting successor.
objective_facts: Simon Boru has terminal pancreatic cancer with only months to live.

Simon was once a criminology researcher who was quietly discredited and dismissed decades ago after a professional betrayal by a mentor at the precinct's founding generation.
Simon has never directly killed anyone himself in this case; each death was staged to implicate someone else while Simon orchestrated from a distance.
Simon's final design requires Elias himself to commit a fatal act against Simon, believing it justified.

case.label: The Puppeteer's Game
case.solution: For nearly a year, Simon Boru — outwardly a courteous antiquarian bookseller — personally committed every murder in the case, staged every crime scene, and mailed every cryptic clue, all as an elaborate act of vengeance for his younger brother Aldous, who was wrongly convicted forty years ago on the confident say-so of another celebrated detective and died in prison still protesting his innocence. Simon fabricated the evidence linking the murders to Han Mei specifically to force Elias into repeating that old injustice, and spent the year's clues deliberately steering Elias toward cutting ethical corners. The killer's true masterwork was never the murders themselves: it was engineering his own death at Elias's hands, alone at the Tannery Docks, off the record and unwitnessed, so that the detective who prided himself on his principles would become, in the one moment that mattered most, exactly the kind of man who destroyed Aldous.
objective_facts: Simon Boru fabricated every clue, staged crime scene, and postcard sent to Elias Vance over the preceding year.
Simon Boru's younger brother, Aldous Boru, was convicted forty years ago of a murder he did not commit and died in prison still protesting his innocence.
The physical evidence linking the murders to Han Mei was planted by Simon Boru and had no genuine connection to Han.
Simon Boru died at the Tannery Docks at Elias Vance's hand, with no other officer present, no arrest made, and no formal record of a confession.
Simon Boru left a handwritten confession in the locked back room of Boru's Rare Books, addressed only to Elias, never filed as evidence.

case.label: The Frame
case.solution: Han Mei was never involved in the murders. Every piece of evidence connecting him to the case — planted in his storeroom and photographed at the scenes — was fabricated by Simon Boru specifically to frame an innocent immigrant shopkeeper, deliberately echoing the wrongful conviction that destroyed Simon's own brother decades earlier. Following Simon's death and the discovery of his private confession, the charges against Han are formally dropped — but the precinct never issues any public correction, leaving Han legally cleared and socially condemned in the same breath.
objective_facts: The physical evidence tying Han Mei to the murders was manufactured by Simon Boru without Han's knowledge.
The charges against Han Mei are formally dropped following the discovery of Simon Boru's confession.
No public statement or press correction clearing Han Mei's name is issued by the Ninth District Precinct.
Graffiti and a boycott of Han's Grocery continue after the charges are quietly dropped.

case.label: The Vance Marriage
case.solution: Elias never had an affair — his secrecy all season was entirely about the killer's game, not another woman — but the emotional abandonment Mira felt was real, and by the season's end his off-the-books killing of Simon Boru becomes one more truth he cannot bring himself to tell her fully. The marriage's future is left genuinely undecided: not reconciled, not ended.

Mira Vance had begun preparing to leave the house before the season's climax.
Elias returns from the Tannery Docks changed in ways he does not explain to Mira.
No reconciliation or separation between Elias and Mira is finalized by the season's end.
